vignette: A 60-year-old male on warfarin presents with profuse epistaxis for 1 hour. He is hemodynamically stable. Direct pressure and topical oxymetazoline have not stopped the bleeding. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Anterior epistaxis (Kiesselbach plexus - most common): Initial management: Direct pressure (lean forward) + Topical vasoconstrictors. If persistent: Anterior nasal packing (Merocel, Rapid Rhino) or chemical cauterization (silver nitrate). Posterior packing if anterior fails or bleeding is posterior.

whyWrong.B: Posterior packing for posterior bleeds (not first-line for anterior).
whyWrong.C: Endoscopic ligation is for refractory bleeding after packing fails.
whyWrong.D: Reversing anticoagulation if life-threatening or packing fails. Not first step.
whyWrong.E: Nasal balloon is alternative packing (similar to anterior packing).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Epistaxis: Direct pressure + Topical vasoconstrictors → Anterior packing → Posterior packing → Surgical/IR intervention.

vignette: A 45-year-old male presents with gradual hearing loss in his left ear. Tuning fork: Rinne test positive in both ears (AC > BC), Weber test localizes to the RIGHT ear. What type of hearing loss is in the left ear?

explanation.whyCorrect: Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL): Rinne positive (AC > BC) in both ears, Weber localizes to the BETTER ear (right). Left ear has SNHL. Left cochlear nerve or inner ear problem. Causes: Acoustic neuroma, Meniere disease, noise-induced, presbycusis, ototoxic drugs.

whyWrong.A: Conductive loss: Rinne negative (BC > AC) on affected side, Weber to affected ear.
whyWrong.C: Mixed: Rinne negative on affected side, Weber variable.
whyWrong.E: Weber to right (better ear) indicates LEFT ear problem, not right.

explanation.clinicalPearl: SNHL: Rinne positive bilaterally + Weber to BETTER ear. Conductive loss: Rinne negative on affected side + Weber to AFFECTED ear.

references: Tuning Fork Tests for Hearing Loss

vignette: A 30-year-old female presents with facial pain, purulent nasal discharge, and fever for 10 days. She had a cold that 'got worse' after 5 days. CT shows air-fluid levels in the maxillary sinuses. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis: Symptoms >10 days OR 'double worsening' (worse after initial improvement). First-line: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125mg BID x5-7 days. Amoxicillin alone is not recommended (H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis produce beta-lactamase).

whyWrong.A: Amoxicillin alone is ineffective due to beta-lactamase-producing organisms.
whyWrong.C: Azithromycin has high resistance rates (not recommended for sinusitis).
whyWrong.D: Saline irrigation is adjunctive, not sole treatment for bacterial sinusitis.
whyWrong.E: Surgery is for chronic sinusitis (>12 weeks) refractory to medical therapy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Bacterial sinusitis: Symptoms >10 days or 'double worsening.' Amoxicillin-clavulanate first-line. CT only if complicated or recurrent.

vignette: A 2-year-old male presents with fever, ear pain, and irritability. He is pulling at his right ear. Otoscopy shows a bulging, erythematous, opaque tympanic membrane with loss of landmarks. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute otitis media (AOM) with severe symptoms (fever >39°C, moderate-severe otalgia): Antibiotics indicated. Amoxicillin 80-90mg/kg/day x10 days (first-line). Observation for 48-72h only for: Age >6mo, non-severe, unilateral, reliable follow-up.

whyWrong.B: Observation only for non-severe AOM with reliable follow-up. This is severe.
whyWrong.C: Azithromycin has high S. pneumoniae resistance (not first-line).
whyWrong.D: Tubes for recurrent AOM (>3 in 6mo or >4 in 1yr) or chronic OME >3mo.
whyWrong.E: Ceftriaxone is for treatment failure or severe penicillin allergy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: AOM: Amoxicillin 80-90mg/kg/day x10 days. Observation ONLY for non-severe >6mo with reliable follow-up.

vignette: A 50-year-old female presents with acute onset severe vertigo, nausea, and vomiting for 6 hours. She cannot walk and prefers to lie still. She has no hearing loss or tinnitus. Dix-Hallpike maneuver is negative. Head impulse test (HIT) shows a corrective saccade on leftward head turn. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Vestibular neuritis: Acute severe vertigo (hours-days) + Nausea/vomiting + Abnormal HIT (corrective saccade) + No hearing loss/tinnitus + Negative Dix-Hallpike. Peripheral etiology (vestibular nerve inflammation). Treatment: Steroids (prednisone taper), vestibular suppressants (meclizine) for first 1-3 days only.

whyWrong.A: BPPV: Brief (<1 min) episodes with position changes, positive Dix-Hallpike, normal HIT.
whyWrong.C: Meniere: Episodic vertigo + Hearing loss + Tinnitus + Aural fullness (hours).
whyWrong.D: Acoustic neuroma: Gradual hearing loss, tinnitus, no acute vertigo.
whyWrong.E: Central vertigo: HINTS exam abnormal (normal HIT, skew deviation, direction-changing nystagmus).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Vestibular neuritis: Acute prolonged vertigo + Abnormal HIT + No hearing loss. Steroids within 72h improve recovery.

vignette: A 20-year-old male presents with severe sore throat (worse on right), fever, 'hot potato' voice, trismus, and uvular deviation to the left. What is the BEST management?

options.text: Needle aspiration or incision and drainage + IV antibiotics

explanation.whyCorrect: Peritonsillar abscess: Trismus + Uvular deviation + Hot potato voice + Severe unilateral sore throat. Treatment: Drainage (needle aspiration, I&D) + IV antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin) + Steroids (reduce inflammation). Most common deep neck infection.

whyWrong.A: Oral antibiotics alone are insufficient for abscess.
whyWrong.C: Tonsillectomy is for recurrent PTA or if drainage fails.
whyWrong.D: Steroids are adjunctive, not sole treatment.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Peritonsillar abscess: Trismus + Uvular deviation + Hot potato voice. Drainage + Antibiotics + Steroids.

vignette: A 25-year-old male presents with seasonal sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itchy eyes every spring. What is the BEST first-line treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Moderate-severe allergic rhinitis: Intranasal corticosteroids (fluticasone, mometasone) are the MOST effective single therapy. Add second-generation antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) for breakthrough symptoms. Avoid first-generation antihistamines (sedation).

whyWrong.A: First-generation antihistamines cause sedation and cognitive impairment.
whyWrong.C: Decongestants alone are less effective and cause rhinitis medicamentosa if used >3 days.
whyWrong.D: Oral steroids for severe refractory cases only (short course).
whyWrong.E: Immunotherapy is for severe/recalcitrant after pharmacotherapy fails.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Allergic rhinitis: Intranasal steroids > Antihistamines. Avoid first-gen antihistamines (sedation). Immunotherapy for refractory.

vignette: A 40-year-old male wakes up with complete hearing loss in his left ear. He has no pain, tinnitus, or vertigo. Tuning fork: Rinne positive bilaterally, Weber localizes to right ear. What is the diagnosis and treatment?

options.text: Meniere disease - Low-salt diet and diuretics
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) - High-dose prednisone

explanation.whyCorrect: SSNHL: Sudden (<72h) sensorineural hearing loss (≥30dB over 3 frequencies). EMERGENCY! High-dose prednisone (60mg/day x7-14 days) within 2 weeks improves recovery. Intratympanic steroids if refractory. MRI to rule out acoustic neuroma (retrocochlear).

whyWrong.A: Meniere: Episodic vertigo + Hearing loss + Tinnitus (not sudden complete loss).
whyWrong.C: Acoustic neuroma: Gradual progressive, not sudden.
whyWrong.D: Eustachian tube: Conductive hearing loss (Rinne negative), not sensorineural.
whyWrong.E: Noise-induced: Gradual, bilateral, history of noise exposure.

explanation.clinicalPearl: SSNHL: Sudden sensorineural loss = EMERGENCY! High-dose prednisone within 2 weeks. MRI to rule out acoustic neuroma.

vignette: A 15-year-old male presents with sore throat, fever 38.5°C, and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Throat exam shows tonsillar exudates and palatal petechiae. Rapid strep test is positive. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Group A Strep pharyngitis: Centor criteria (fever, exudates, lymphadenopathy, no cough) + Positive rapid strep. Amoxicillin or Penicillin V x10 days (first-line). Goal: Prevent rheumatic fever (not for symptom relief). Azithromycin for penicillin-allergic patients.

whyWrong.B: Macrolides have increasing resistance (use only if penicillin-allergic).
whyWrong.C: Cephalosporins are alternative but penicillin is first-line.
whyWrong.E: Positive rapid strep requires antibiotics to prevent rheumatic fever.

explanation.clinicalPearl: GAS pharyngitis: Penicillin/Amoxicillin x10 days. Prevents rheumatic fever (not just symptom relief).

vignette: A 60-year-old male with 50 pack-year smoking history and heavy alcohol use presents with hoarseness for 3 months. He has no pain. What is the BEST next step?

options.text: Laryngoscopy with biopsy of any suspicious lesions
Voice rest and follow-up in 3 months

explanation.whyCorrect: Hoarseness >3 weeks in smoker/drinker = RED FLAG for laryngeal cancer. Laryngoscopy (flexible or direct) with biopsy is mandatory. 95% are squamous cell carcinoma. Early stage: Radiation or laser excision. Advanced: Chemoradiation or total laryngectomy.

whyWrong.A: CXR does not evaluate laryngeal pathology.
whyWrong.C: PPI trial for reflux (max 4-8 weeks). Persistent hoarseness >2-3 weeks = scope.
whyWrong.D: Hoarseness >3 weeks requires laryngoscopy, not observation.
whyWrong.E: CT is for staging after diagnosis, not initial evaluation.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hoarseness >3 weeks + Smoking/Alcohol = Laryngoscopy to rule out cancer. 95% are SCC.

vignette: A 2-year-old boy is brought in with sudden onset coughing, stridor, and wheezing after eating peanuts. He is in mild respiratory distress. CXR shows hyperinflation of the right lung. What is the BEST management?

options.text: Rigid bronchoscopy for foreign body removal

explanation.whyCorrect: Foreign body aspiration: Ball-valve effect → Hyperinflation (air enters, cannot exit). Rigid bronchoscopy = Gold standard for diagnosis AND treatment. Flexible bronchoscopy for distal foreign bodies. If complete obstruction (no breath sounds, severe distress) → Back blows/chest thrusts in infants.

whyWrong.A: Bronchodilators do not relieve mechanical obstruction.
whyWrong.C: Heimlich for complete obstruction, not partial with stridor.
whyWrong.D: Chest PT may dislodge FB and worsen obstruction.
whyWrong.E: Steroids may reduce edema but do not remove FB.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Foreign body aspiration: Hyperinflation on CXR + Stridor/Cough. Rigid bronchoscopy = Diagnostic + Therapeutic.

vignette: A 35-year-old female presents with sudden onset right-sided facial weakness involving the forehead, inability to close her right eye, and loss of the nasolabial fold. She has no other neurological symptoms. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Bell's palsy (LMN facial nerve palsy): Forehead INVOLVED (differentiates from UMN stroke). Treatment: Prednisone within 72h (improves recovery) + Valacyclovir (if severe, may improve outcomes). Eye protection (artificial tears, taping eye at night). Most recover completely in 3-6 months.

whyWrong.C: Antivirals may provide additional benefit (controversial but commonly given).
whyWrong.D: Surgery is last resort for severe persistent cases.
whyWrong.E: Antivirals alone are insufficient (steroids are the mainstay).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Bell's palsy: Forehead INVOLVED = LMN. Forehead spared = UMN (stroke). Prednisone within 72h improves recovery.
